text: This is a video of a sixty-two year old male with distal pancreatic mass measuring six by five centimeters. The patient had EOS complicated with perforation and peripancreatic inflammation and fluid collection. Pathology from EUS showed the neuroendocrine tumor. The reason for performing EUS in the presence of a mass this big was that the patient had the lesion, a small lesion in his lung, and we wanted to rule out adenocarcinoma of the pancreas. Again, we can see the lesion here and we can see the close association between the DJ, flexure and the pancreatic mass. The colon was not in that close proximity to the inflammation and the spleen was really enlarged compared to the size of the liver. Patient has no history of portal hypertension

This is a video of a sixty-two year old male with distal pancreatic mass measuring six by five centimeters. The patient had EOS complicated with perforation and peripancreatic inflammation and fluid collection. Pathology from EUS showed the neuroendocrine tumor. The reason for performing EUS in the presence of a mass this big was that the patient had the lesion, a small lesion in his lung, and we wanted to rule out adenocarcinoma of the pancreas. Again, we can see the lesion here and we can see the close association between the DJ, flexure and the pancreatic mass. The colon was not in that close proximity to the inflammation and the spleen was really enlarged compared to the size of the liver. Patient has no history of portal hypertension

The robotic surgery begins with the opening of the gastro-colic omentum, allowing entry into the lesser sac. A vessel sealer is used to further open the gastrocolic omentum, retracting the colon downward to expose the pancreas and the lesser sac.

The robotic surgery begins with the opening of the gastro-colic omentum, entering the lesser sac. A vessel sealer is used to further open the omentum, bringing down the colon to expose the pancreas and lesser sac. The gastrocolic omentum is opened towards the distal stomach, near the pylorus. Adhesions between the stomach and pancreas are visible, resulting from previous perforation. The surgeon enters the inflammatory cavity between the pancreas and stomach, draining sterile fluid from the cavity.

The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum towards the distal stomach, approaching the pylorus. Adhesions between the stomach and pancreas are visible, resulting from previous perforation. The inflammatory cavity between the pancreas and stomach is entered, and fluid is drained from the cavity.

The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar device to carefully dissect through necrotic and inflammatory tissue, mobilizing the colon and exposing the inferior border of the pancreas. The colon is gradually brought down, avoiding injury, while adhesions are meticulously taken down.

The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar device to carefully dissect through inflamed tissue, mobilizing the colon and exposing the inferior border of the pancreas. Necrotic tissue is visible, and adhesions are being taken down to reveal the pancreas amidst significant inflammation.
The surgeon visualizes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz and performs careful lasso adhesion using cold scissors to separate the tissue.

The surgeon visualizes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz, performing careful lasso adhesion with cold scissors. The colon is inspected, and the middle colic artery is visualized, indicating the location of the SMV and portal vein. Adhesions are further dissected until the duodenal flexure is separated from the inferior border of the pancreas. Dissection continues beneath the pancreas towards the retropancreatic plane, which typically requires minimal dissection in a normal pancreas.

The surgeon inspects the colon and visualizes the middle colic artery, providing a reference for the location of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein. Adhesions are further lysed until the duodenal flexure is completely separated from the inferior border of the pancreas. Dissection continues beneath the pancreas towards the retropancreatic plane, which typically requires minimal dissection in a normal pancreas.

The pancreas is lifted using a tip-up fenestrated instrument while a laparoscopic ultrasound probe confirms the tumor location. The surgeon frees the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas to dissect the splenic artery. A vessel sealer is used to remove the inflammatory wall between the stomach and pancreas. The robotic system provides ten-time magnification for clear visualization, aiding in careful dissection. A robotic suction irrigator assists in lysing adhesions and suctioning, with the colon being gently pulled away from the surgical field.

The pancreas is lifted using a tip-up fenestrated instrument while a laparoscopic ultrasound probe confirms the tumor location. The surgeon frees the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas to dissect the splenic artery, using a vessel sealer to remove the inflammatory wall between the stomach and pancreas. The robotic suction irrigator aids in adhesion removal and suctioning, with the colon being gently pulled away from potential harm.

The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to enter the inflammatory cavity, suctioning the fluid within. The colon is safely repositioned, and attention shifts to forming the retropancreatic tunnel. The inferior pancreatic artery, arising from the SMA, is identified, clipped, and transected.

The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to enter the inflammatory cavity, suctioning fluid from the cavity. The colon is safely repositioned, and attention shifts to forming the retropancreatic tunnel. The inferior pancreatic artery, arising from the SMA, is identified, clipped, and transected.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery located on the superior border of the pancreas, carefully dissecting through a thick inflammatory peel. The dissection continues to ensure the artery is correctly identified and not mistaken for the common hepatic artery, confirming its path towards the pancreas.

The surgeon attempts to expose the splenic artery located on the superior border of the pancreas, which is covered by a thick inflammatory peel. Careful dissection is performed to ensure the artery is correctly identified and not mistaken for the common hepatic artery, with the dissection continuing towards the pancreas to confirm its path.

The surgeon clamps the splenic artery, observing changes in liver color to ensure blood flow is not compromised, and inspects the spleen for signs of ischemia. Dissection beneath the artery is performed to encircle it with a vessel loop, which is then placed. The surgeon attempts dissection towards the common hepatic artery, encountering difficulty due to severe inflammation.

The surgeon clamps the artery, observing changes in liver color to ensure blood flow is not compromised, while inspecting the spleen for signs of ischemia. Dissection beneath the artery is performed, encircling it with a vessel loop. The vessel loop is placed, and dissection towards the common hepatic artery is attempted, though challenging due to severe inflammation.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein behind the pancreas and forms a tunnel between the splenic vein and the pancreas. A vessel loop is passed around the splenic vein. Dissection is carried out towards the pancreas to confirm that the looped vessel is the splenic artery, ensuring it is not the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein behind the pancreas and forms a tunnel between the vein and the pancreas. A vessel loop is passed around the splenic artery, confirming it is not the common hepatic artery. The robotic vascular stapler is used to transect the splenic artery, and the vessel loop is removed. The splenic vein is clipped and transected. The procedure involves a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy without spleen preservation due to tumor size.

The surgeon clips and transects the splenic vein. A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it, and a green load 45 stapler is used to clamp and transect the pancreas. The stapler is adjusted for appropriate thickness before firing, ensuring the vessel loop remains on the specimen side.

A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it, facilitating the use of a green load 45 stapler. The pancreas is clamped with the stapler, which uses smart technology to adjust for thickness. After achieving the appropriate clamping thickness, the clamp is held for three minutes before firing, resulting in the transection of the pancreas. The vessel loop remains on the specimen side, avoiding the need for additional resection.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral fashion using a vessel sealer and Maryland bipolar. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and the splenic attachment to the diaphragm is detached. The stomach is submerged in saline to check for injuries, appearing intact, and the colon is inspected for damage. A drain is placed in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral fashion using a vessel sealer and Maryland bipolar forceps. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and the entire stomach is mobilized. The splenic attachment to the diaphragm is dissected. After the specimen removal, the stomach is submerged in saline to check for injuries, appearing intact. The colon is inspected for injuries, and a drain is placed in the left upper quadrant.
